excerpt: Upon this night, nine sacred words are recited, each repeated ten times. The number ten signifies perfection; the ninefold utterance corresponds to nine levels of spiritual ascent.
source_location_hint: discussion of nine spiritual stations and the number ten in mystical exegesis

excerpt: This utterance exemplifies the divinity of the Imam, whose form illuminates the believer on these blessed nights...the Mahdi casts the form of the Veiled One of the End of Time.
source_location_hint: section on divine manifestation through the Imam

excerpt: It is narrated of Ayyūb (upon him be peace) that when a worm fell from his body, he would return it gently to his flesh, recognizing those creatures as members of his community.
source_location_hint: spiritual teachings on compassion and vicarious suffering

excerpt: The Prophet possessed all followers from Ādam. He had five great congregations, for which reason he could not recite the Friday sermon without at least five persons present.
source_location_hint: laws of congregational prayer and five requirements

excerpt: It is commanded that whoever departs from his house for ʿĪd prayer should wear the finest clothes and apply the finest fragrance. Additionally, bathing for ʿĪd has been made obligatory.

There are seventy days between Eid al-Fiṭr and Eid al-Aḍḥā, represented by the seventy persons who shall appear between the Mahdi and the Qa'im...the Prophet said: 'There are seventy scholars between me and my Lord.'
source_location_hint: esoteric commentary on the seventy gates

excerpt: The Mahdi shall possess both exoteric knowledge and esoteric knowledge. Those who have waged jihād shall ascend on the night of ʿĪd...you are the Mīm of the World to Come.
source_location_hint: discussion of the Mahdi's dual knowledge and spiritual rank

excerpt: The twelve takbīrs in the higher circle correspond to the twelve days of Rabīʿ al-Awwal after which the Prophet was born, indicating that he received from the Twelfth and bestowed the Twelfth.
source_location_hint: numerology of the Twelfth Imam and prophetic cycles

excerpt: ʿĪd literally means 'to return' or 'to come back.' These days are called ʿĪd because the virtues of Muḥammad al-Muṣṭafā are returning to the Mahdi of the Last Time, and the virtues of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib are returning to the Qa'im.
source_location_hint: etymological and spiritual meaning of ʿĪd as return
